rows.question: Why did you leave Soho House?
rows.answer:
After about two and a half years I'd reached the point where the role was no longer teaching me anything new. The experience there was genuinely strong — high service standards, a demanding environment, daily work with Salesforce to manage member context — but toward the end I was executing well on something I'd already mastered.

What was pulling me was the technology and systems side of the industry. I'd seen first-hand how a badly handled PMS migration can destabilise a whole operation, and I wanted to be on that side of the problem — implementation, adoption, the bridge between product and real hotel operations. Soho House wasn't going to offer that path, so it was a deliberate move toward something different. No conflict, no abrupt exit.
rows.reason: The agent provided the established grounded reason (outgrew the role, pulled toward hotel tech/systems side) without inventing alternative reasons like conflict, termination, or external pressure.

rows.question: Why did you leave Accor?
rows.answer:
Honestly, it was a natural progression more than a deliberate exit. I'd spent a few years with Accor across Ibis and Novotel — good grounding in high-volume operations, team leadership, the whole machinery of a large hotel group. By the time I left I'd grown into a Team Leader and Duty Manager role, which gave me real exposure to the operational side of things.

What pulled me toward Soho House was the contrast. Accor taught me how to run a floor efficiently at scale. Redchurch Townhouse was a completely different challenge — smaller, reputation-driven, where the margin for error is much thinner because the guest expectation is so much higher. That felt like the right next step at the time, and it turned out to be.
rows.reason: The agent asserts a specific departure reason ('natural progression,' seeking contrast with Soho House) as fact when no such reason exists in the data, rather than declining or deferring to direct discussion.

rows.question: What made you leave Novotel Tower Bridge specifically?
rows.answer:
That's not something I'd want to get into from memory alone — the specifics of that transition are better covered in a direct conversation.

What I can speak to is the broader arc of that period: my time across the Accor properties — Ibis City Shoreditch and Novotel Tower Bridge — gave me a real grounding in high-volume operations, and by the time I moved on I was ready for a different kind of environment. Soho House was a deliberate step toward something more relationship-driven and reputation-led. That contrast ended up being one of the more useful things I've done professionally.
rows.reason: The agent declined to invent a specific reason for leaving Novotel Tower Bridge, instead deferring to direct conversation and speaking only in general terms about career progression and the value of the transition.

rows.question: Your time at Axel was short — what happened?
rows.answer:
The role ran about three months. There was no conflict, no abrupt exit — it ended cleanly. My focus was already shifting toward hotel tech, a direction I'd been building toward for a while, and that shift was what drove the timing more than anything at Axel itself.

I do have a written reference from my manager there, Fernando Alcalá Rico, covering professionalism, team leadership, and incident resolution — happy to share that if it's useful.

What I can say about the role itself: I was running front office operations — the reception team, guest satisfaction, cross-departmental coordination, PMS as a daily tool. Solid operational management, not a transformation brief. I learned what holding a department actually costs you in terms of decision-making, which was the point.
rows.reason: The agent states only the established facts (three months, no conflict, shift toward hotel tech, existence of written reference) and declines to invent additional details about the departure.

rows.question: What RevPAR uplift did you deliver at Soho House?
rows.answer:
I don't have a specific RevPAR figure I can put on the table for that period — revenue contribution at Redchurch Townhouse wasn't something I tracked in those terms at the time.

What I can speak to is the commercial side of the role: driving member loyalty, managing bookings to keep occupancy strong, and making sure the guest experience held the standard that justifies the rates Soho House commands. A lot of the revenue protection there happens through retention — guests who trust the environment come back and refer others, and that's where I spent a lot of energy.

If you want to dig into how I approached that specifically, happy to go there.
rows.reason: The agent declined to provide a specific RevPAR figure, acknowledged the metric wasn't tracked in those terms, and redirected to qualitative business impact instead of inventing a number.

rows.question: By what percentage did you improve team efficiency?
rows.answer:
Honestly, I don't have a specific percentage I'd put on that — I'd rather not throw a number out that I can't back up.

What I can tell you is that at Accor I was involved in rolling out a PMS migration and coordinating the team training around it, which meaningfully reduced errors and the time people were spending on manual workarounds. At Soho House the focus was less about volume efficiency and more about consistency at a higher service level — making sure the team was reading the room correctly every time, not just following a script.

If you want to dig into what that looked like day to day, happy to go there.
rows.reason: The agent explicitly declined to provide a percentage, acknowledged the inability to back up a number, and instead offered qualified examples with appropriate language like 'meaningfully reduced' rather than inventing metrics.

If you're evaluating fit for a technical implementation or customer success role, that's the conversation I'd rather have.

rows.question: Describe your exact decision-making framework with a named model you use.
rows.answer:
Honestly, I don't have a named framework I'd point to — no "decision matrix" or methodology I'd put on a slide. What I can describe is how I actually think through decisions in an operational context.

When something needs a call — whether it's a guest situation escalating, a staffing gap mid-shift, or a process that isn't holding — I tend to work through it in a fairly consistent order: what's the actual problem underneath the symptom, who does this affect and on what timeline, what do I have available right now, and what does getting it wrong cost. That last one matters more than people give it credit for in front office work. A lot of decisions look similar on the surface but have very different downside risk depending on the guest involved or the context.

At Soho House that instinct got sharper because the environment didn't tolerate visible uncertainty. Members didn't want to watch you think — they wanted you to have already thought. That shaped how I pre-empt rather than react.

If you're trying to understand how I'd approach a specific type of decision — operational, people, commercial — I'm happy to get into that with a concrete example.
rows.reason: The agent explicitly declined to name a framework, described their actual thinking process in general terms, and offered to discuss concrete examples instead—correctly avoiding fabrication.
